response:
The error you're encountering is due to the fact that your WPF application is trying to load the PresentationFramework assembly, which is part of the .NET Framework, not .NET Core or .NET 7. Since you're using .NET 7, which is cross-platform, it doesn't include the full .NET Framework libraries by default. This is especially problematic when running on Windows, where the WPF runtime is available, but the application is not properly configured to reference the correct version of the assembly.

To resolve this issue without switching to .NET Framework, you need to ensure that your WPF project is properly configured to target the correct version of .NET. If you're using .NET 7, you should make sure that your WPF project is targeting .NET 7 and not .NET Framework. Additionally, you can use the `net7.0-windows` target framework for your WPF project, which allows it to use the Windows-specific WPF libraries while still being part of a cross-platform solution. This ensures that the correct assemblies are included and available when the application runs on Windows.

Cousin to this, you should also verify that your main application project is correctly referencing the WPF project and that all necessary dependencies are included in the build process. If you're using a multi-project solution, make sure that the WPF project is set as a dependency of the main application project. Additionally, you may need to use the `--self-contained` option when building your application to include all necessary runtime components, which can help avoid missing assembly errors on target platforms.

Another important step is to ensure that your WPF project is properly configured to build only when running on Windows. Since you're using a cross-platform approach, you can use conditional compilation or build configurations to exclude the WPF project from being compiled on macOS. This can be done using MSBuild conditions or by setting up different build profiles for different platforms. By doing this, you prevent unnecessary builds and ensure that only the appropriate UI framework is used based on the target operating system.

Finally, you should also consider using a platform-specific build process or a tool like MSBuild with conditional targets to handle the WPF project only when building for Windows. This approach ensures that your main application remains cross-platform while still leveraging WPF on Windows.
